text:
Every year, each student in a nationally representative sample is given tests in various subjects. Recently, a 
random sample of 9,600 twelfth-grade students from the United States were administered a multiple-choice 
United States history exam. One of the multiple-choice questions is below. (The correct answer is C.) 
 
In 1935 and 1936 the Supreme Court declared that important parts of the New Deal were unconstitutional. 
President Roosevelt responded by threatening to

options.A: impeach several Supreme Court justices
options.B: eliminate the Supreme Court
options.C: appoint additional Supreme Court justices who shared his views
options.D:
override the Supreme Court's decisions by gaining three-fourths majorities in both houses of 
Congress 
 
 Of the 9,600 students, 28 percent answered the multiple-choice question correctly.

text: Of the following dotplots, which represents the set of data that has the greatest standard deviation?
A random sample of 374 United States pennies was collected, and the age of each penny was determined. According to the boxplot below, what is the approximate interquartile range (IQR) of the ages?
The histogram above shows the number of minutes needed by 45 students to finish playing a computer game. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: The distribution is skewed to the right.
options.B: The distribution is skewed to the left.
options.C: The distribution appears to be normal.
options.D: The distribution appears to be chi-square.

text: A bank surveyed all of its 60 employees to determine the proportion who participate in volunteer activities. Which of the following statements is true?

options.A: The bank should not use the data from this survey because this is an observational study.
options.B: The bank can use the result of this survey to prove that working for the bank causes employees to participate in volunteer activities.
options.C: The bank did not select a random sample of employees, so the survey will not provide the bank with useful information.
options.D: The bank would have to use the survey data to construct a confidence interval in order to estimate the proportion of employees who participate in volunteer activities.

text: The histogram below displays the frequencies of waiting times, in minutes, for 175 patients in a dentist’s office. Which of the following could be the median of the waiting times, in minutes?

Data were collected on the amount, in dollars, that individual customers spent on dinner in an Italian restaurant. The quartiles for these data are given below. Q1 Q2 Q3 $36.27 $44.27 $58.97 Which of the following statements must be true for these customers?

options.A: At least half of the customers spent less than or equal to $44.27 and at least half spent greater than or equal to $44.27.
options.B: Seventy-five percent of the customers spent between $36.27 and $58.97.
options.C: Twenty-five percent of the customers spent less than or equal to $58.97 and the remaining 75 percent spent greater than or equal to $58.97.
options.D: The mean amount spent by customers is $44.27.

text: The weight of adult male grizzly bears living in the wild in the continental United States is approximately normally distributed with a mean of 500 pounds and a standard deviation of 50 pounds. The weight of adult female grizzly bears is approximately normally distributed with a mean of 300 pounds and a standard deviation of 40 pounds. Approximately, what would be the weight of a female grizzly bear with the same standardized score (z-score) as a male grizzly bear with a weight of 530 pounds?

options.A: 276 pounds
options.B: 324 pounds
options.C: 330 pounds
options.D: 340 pounds

text: A company sells concrete in batches of 5 cubic yards. The probability distribution of X, the number of cubic yards sold in a single order for concrete from this company, is shown in the table below. X = the number of cubic yards 10 15 20 25 30 Probability 0.15 0.25 0.25 0.30 0.05 The expected value of the probability distribution of X is 19.25 and the standard deviation is 5.76. There is a fixed cost to deliver the concrete. The profit Y, in dollars, for a particular order can be described by 100. Y X = - What is the standard deviation of Y ?

Based on a survey of a random sample of 900 adults in the United States, a journalist reports that 60 percent of adults in the United States are in favor of increasing the minimum hourly wage. If the reported percent has a margin of error of 2.7 percentage points, which of the following is closest to the level of confidence?

A compact disc (CD) manufacturer wanted to determine which of two different cover designs for a newly released CD will generate more sales. The manufacturer chose 70 stores to sell the CD. Thirty-five of these stores were randomly assigned to sell CDs with one of the cover designs and the other 35 were assigned to sell the CDs with the other cover design. The manufacturer recorded the number of CDs sold at each of the stores and found a significant difference between the mean number of CDs sold for the two cover designs. Which of the following gives the conclusion that should be made based on the results and provides the best explanation for the conclusion?

options.A: It is not reasonable to conclude that the difference in sales was caused by the different cover designs because this was not an experiment.
options.B: It is not reasonable to conclude that the difference in sales was caused by the different cover designs because there was no control group for comparison.
options.C: It is not reasonable to conclude that the difference in sales was caused by the different cover designs because the 70 stores were not randomly chosen.
options.D: It is reasonable to conclude that the difference in sales was caused by the different cover designs because the cover designs were randomly assigned to stores.

text: The manager of a public swimming pool wants to compare the effectiveness of two laundry detergents, Detergent A and Detergent B, in cleaning the towels that are used daily. As each dirty towel is turned in, it is placed into the only washing machine on the premises. When the washing machine contains 20 towels, the manager flips a coin to determine whether Detergent A or Detergent B will be used for that load. The cleanliness of the load of towels is rated on a scale of 1 to 10 by a person who does not know which detergent was used. The manager continues this experiment for many days. Which of the following best describes the manager’s study?

options.A: A completely randomized design
options.B: A randomized block design with Detergent A and Detergent B as blocks
options.C: A randomized block design with the washing machine as the block
options.D: A matched-pairs design with Detergent A and Detergent B as the pair

text: In the design of a survey, which of the following best explains how to minimize response bias?

options.A: Increase the sample size.
options.B: Decrease the sample size.
options.C: Randomly select the sample.
options.D: Increase the number of questions in the survey.
options.E: Carefully word and field-test survey questions.

text: For a sample of 42 rabbits, the mean weight is 5 pounds and the standard deviation of weights is 3 pounds. Which of the following is most likely true about the weights for the rabbits in this sample?

options.A: The distribution of weights is approximately normal because the sample size is 42, and therefore the central limit theorem applies.
options.B: The distribution of weights is approximately normal because the standard deviation is less than the mean.
options.C: The distribution of weights is skewed to the right because the least possible weight is within 2 standard deviations of the mean.
options.D: The distribution of weights is skewed to the left because the least possible weight is within 2 standard deviations of the mean.

text: When conducting a large sample test of 0 H : p p = for a single proportion, the test statistic is ( ) ( ) 0 ˆ , p p z p p n - = - where ˆp is the sample proportion. Which of the following best explains the justification for the denominator of this test statistic?

options.A: The standard deviation of ˆp is known when the null hypothesis is true.
options.B: The standard deviation of ˆp is known when the alternative hypothesis is true.
options.C: The sample size is large and therefore the standard deviation of p is approximated well.
options.D: The standard deviation of p is known when the null hypothesis is true.

text: A polling firm is interested in surveying a representative sample of registered voters in the United States. The firm has automated its sampling so that random phone numbers within the United States are called. Each time a number is called, the procedure below is followed. • If there is no response or if an answering machine is reached, another number is automatically called. • If a person answers, a survey worker verifies that the person is at least 18 years of age. • If the person is not at least 18 years of age, no response is recorded, and another number is called. • If the person is at least 18 years of age, that person is surveyed. Some people claim the procedure being used does not permit the results to be extended to all registered voters. Which of the following is NOT a legitimate concern about the procedure being used?

options.A: Registered voters with children under the age of 18 years may be underrepresented in the sample.
options.B: Registered voters with unlisted telephone numbers may be underrepresented in the sample.
options.C: Registered voters who have more than one telephone number may be overrepresented in the sample.
options.D: Registered voters who live in households consisting of more than one voter may be underrepresented.

text: A complex electronic device contains three components, A, B, and C. The probabilities of failure for each component in any one year are 0.01, 0.03, and 0.04, respectively. If any one component fails, the device will fail. If the components fail independently of one another, what is the probability that the device will not fail in one year?

options.A: Less than 0.01
options.E: Greater than 0.99

text: A large-sample 98 percent confidence interval for the proportion of hotel reservations that are canceled on the intended arrival day is (0.048, 0.112). What is the point estimate for the proportion of hotel reservations that are canceled on the intended arrival day from which this interval was constructed?

When using a one-sample t-procedure to construct a confidence interval for the mean of a finite population, a condition is that the population size be at least 10 times the sample size. The reason for the condition is to ensure that

options.A: the sample size is large enough
options.B: the central limit theorem is applicable for the sample mean
options.C: the sample standard deviation is a good approximation of the population standard deviation
options.D: the degree of dependence among observations is negligible

text: A nonprofit organization plans to hold a raffle to raise funds for its operations. A total of 1,000 raffle tickets will be sold for $1.00 each. After all the tickets are sold, one ticket will be selected at random and its owner will receive $50.00. The expected value for the net gain for each ticket is $0.95. - What is the meaning of the expected value in this context?

options.A: The ticket owners lose an average of $0.05 per raffle ticket.
options.B: The ticket owners lose an average of $0.95 per raffle ticket.
options.C: Each ticket owner will lose $0.95 per raffle ticket.
options.D: A ticket owner would have to purchase 19 more tickets for the expected value of his or her net gain to increase to $0.00.
options.E: A ticket owner has a 95 percent chance of having a ticket that is not selected.

text: Suppose that on a hypothesis test for a single population mean, Ha: μ < 10. Assume that Ha is true. For a fixed sample size and significance level α, the power of the test will be greatest if the actual mean is which of the following?

The residual plots from five different least squares regression lines are shown below. Which of the plots provides the strongest evidence that its regression line is an appropriate model for the data and is consistent with the assumptions required for inference for regression?

A random sample of 50 students at a large high school resulted in a 95 percent confidence interval for the mean number of hours of sleep per day of (6.73, 7.67). Which of the following statements best summarizes the meaning of this confidence interval?

options.A: About 95% of all random samples of 50 students from this population would result in a 95% confidence interval of (6.73, 7.67).
options.B: About 95% of all random samples of 50 students from this population would result in a 95% confidence interval that covered the population mean number of hours of sleep per day.
options.C: 95% of the students in the survey reported sleeping between 6.73 and 7.67 hours per day.
options.D: 95% of the students in this high school sleep between 6.73 and 7.67 hours per day.

text: A local company is interested in supporting environmentally friendly initiatives such as carpooling among employees. The company surveyed all of the 200 employees at the downtown offices. Employees responded as to whether or not they own a car and to the location of the home where they live. The results are shown in the table below. Location of Home Downtown Area In the City Elsewhere In the City Outside the City Total Yes 10 15 35 60 No 60 55 25 140 Car Ownership Total 70 70 60 200 Which of the following statements about a randomly chosen person from these 200 employees is true?

options.A: If the person owns a car, he or she is more likely to live elsewhere in the city than to live in the downtown area in the city.
options.B: If the person does not own a car, he or she is more likely to live outside the city than to live in the city (downtown area or elsewhere).
options.C: The person is more likely to own a car if he or she lives in the city (downtown area or elsewhere) than if he or she lives outside the city.
options.D: The person is more likely to live in the downtown area in the city than elsewhere in the city.

text: A random sample of 432 voters revealed that 100 are in favor of a certain bond issue. A 95 percent confidence interval for the proportion of the population of voters who are in favor of the bond issue is

The commuting time for a student to travel from home to a college campus is normally distributed with a mean of 30 minutes and a standard deviation of 5 minutes. If the student leaves home at 8:25 A.M., what is the probability that the student will arrive at the college campus later than 9 A.M.?

In 2009 a survey of Internet usage found that 79 percent of adults age 18 years and older in the United States use the Internet. A broadband company believes that the percent is greater now than it was in 2009 and will conduct a survey. The company plans to construct a 98 percent confidence interval to estimate the current percent and wants the margin of error to be no more than 2.5 percentage points. Assuming that at least 79 percent of adults use the Internet, which of the following should be used to find the sample size (n) needed?

A manufacturer claims its Brand A battery lasts longer than its competitor’s Brand B battery. Nine batteries of each brand are tested independently, and the hours of battery life are shown in the table below. Brand A 88 85 80 81 72 90 85 85 84 Brand B 80 79 77 82 75 81 77 73 78 Provided that the assumptions for inference are met, which of the following tests should be conducted to determine if Brand A batteries do, in fact, last longer than Brand B batteries?

options.A: A one-sided, paired t-test
options.B: A one-sided, two-sample t-test
options.C: A two-sided, two-sample t-test
options.D: A one-sided, two-sample z-test

text: An experimenter conducted a two-tailed hypothesis test on a set of data and obtained a p-value of 0.44. If the experimenter had conducted a one-tailed test on the same set of data, which of the following is true about the possible p-value(s) that the experimenter could have obtained?

options.A: The only possible p-value is 0.22.
options.B: The only possible p-value is 0.44.
options.C: The only possible p-value is 0.88.
options.D: The possible p-values are 0.22 and 0.78.

text: A randomized experiment was performed to determine whether two fertilizers, A and B, give different yields of tomatoes. A total of 33 tomato plants were grown; 16 using fertilizer A, and 17 using fertilizer B. The distributions of the data did not show marked skewness and there were no outliers in either data set. The results of the experiment are shown below. Fertilizer A Fertilizer B Average number of tomatoes per plant 19.54 23.39 Standard deviation 3.68 4.93 Number of plants 16 17 Which of the following statements best describes the conclusion that can be drawn from this experiment?

options.A: There is no statistical evidence of difference in the yields between fertilizer A and fertilizer B ( ) 0.15 . > p
options.B: There is a borderline statistically significant difference in the yields between fertilizer A and fertilizer B ( ) 0.10 0.15 . < < p
options.C: There is evidence of a statistically significant difference in the yields between fertilizer A and fertilizer B ( ) 0.05 0.10 . < < p
options.D: There is evidence of a statistically significant difference in the yields between fertilizer A and fertilizer B ( ) 0.01 0.05 . < < p

text: In order to plan its next advertising campaign, the Trendy Motor Vehicle Company is investigating whether the type of vehicle and the color of vehicle are related. Each person in a random sample of size 275 selected from the company’s mailing list was classified according to the type (car or truck) and the color of vehicle he or she drove. The data are shown in the table below. Vehicle Color Red Black White Tan Green Car 35 23 41 21 12 Vehicle Type Truck 27 55 39 12 10 Which of the following procedures would be most appropriate to use for investigating whether there is a relationship between vehicle type and color?

options.A: A two-sample t-test
options.B: A two-sample z-test
options.C: A matched pairs t-test
options.D: A chi-square goodness-of-fit test

text: A large number of randomized experiments were conducted to determine whether taking a particular drug regularly would decrease the chance of getting a certain disease. For each of the experiments, the drug effect is the difference between the proportion of people taking the drug who got the disease and the proportion of people taking a placebo who got the disease. If the drug had no effect whatsoever, which of the following experimental results would be anticipated? I. p-values will be greater than 0.05 for about 95 percent of the experiments. II. There will be about an equal number of experiments showing positive and negative values of drug effect. III. When 95 percent confidence intervals for the population drug effect are constructed, those confidence intervals include 0 about 95 percent of the time.

options.A: I only
options.B: II only
options.C: III only
options.D: I and II only

text: As part of a class project at a large university, Amber selected a random sample of 12 students in her major field of study. All students in the sample were asked to report their number of hours spent studying for the final exam and their score on the final exam. A regression analysis on the data produced the following partial computer output. Predictor Coef SE Coef T P Constant 62.328 4.570 13.64 0.000 Study Hours 2.697 0.745 3.62 0.005 S = 5.505 R-sq = 56.7% Amber wants to compute a 95 percent confidence interval for the slope of the least squares regression line in the population of all students in her major field of study. Assuming that conditions for inference are satisfied, which of the following gives the margin of error for the confidence interval?

A mathematics competition uses the following scoring procedure to discourage students from guessing (choosing an answer randomly) on the multiple-choice questions. For each correct response, the score is 7. For each question left unanswered, the score is 2. For each incorrect response, the score is 0. If there are 5 choices for each question, what is the minimum number of choices that the student must eliminate before it is advantageous to guess among the rest?

The probability that a new microwave oven will stop working in less than 2 years is 0.05. The probability that a new microwave oven is damaged during delivery and stops working in less than 2 years is 0.04. The probability that a new microwave oven is damaged during delivery is 0.10. Given that a new microwave oven is damaged during delivery, what is the probability that it stops working in less than 2 years?

Perchlorate is a chemical used in rocket fuel. People who live near a former rocket-testing site are concerned that perchlorate is present in unsafe amounts in their drinking water. Drinking water is considered safe when the average level of perchlorate is 24.5 parts per billion (ppb) or less. A random sample of 28 water sources in this area produces a mean perchlorate measure of 25.3 ppb. Which of the following is an appropriate alternative hypothesis that addresses their concern?

The computer output below shows the result of a linear regression analysis for predicting the concentration of zinc, in parts per million (ppm), from the concentration of lead, in ppm, found in fish from a certain river. Response variable is Zinc (ppm) Variable Coefficient Std Dev T P Constant 16.3 4.90 3.32 0.003 Lead (ppm) 19.0 1.89 10.01 0.000 S = 16.17 R-Sq = 82.0% Which of the following statements is a correct interpretation of the value 19.0 in the output?

options.A: On average there is a predicted increase of 19.0 ppm in concentration of lead for every increase of 1 ppm in concentration of zinc found in the fish.
options.B: On average there is a predicted increase of 19.0 ppm in concentration of zinc for every increase of 1 ppm in concentration of lead found in the fish.
options.C: The predicted concentration of zinc is 19.0 ppm in fish with no concentration of lead.
options.D: The predicted concentration of lead is 19.0 ppm in fish with no concentration of zinc.

text: There were 5,317 previously owned homes sold in a western city in the year 2000. The distribution of the sales prices of these homes was strongly right-skewed, with a mean of $206,274 and a standard deviation of $37,881. If all possible simple random samples of size 100 are drawn from this population and the mean is computed for each of these samples, which of the following describes the sampling distribution of the sample mean?

options.A: Approximately normal with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $3,788
options.B: Approximately normal with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $37,881
options.C: Approximately normal with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $520
options.D: Strongly right-skewed with mean $206,274 and standard deviation $3,788

text: A physician believes that the exercise habits of East Coast adults are different from the exercise habits of West Coast adults. To study this, she gathers information on the number of hours of exercise per week from a random sample of East Coast adults and a random sample of West Coast adults. Which of the following might be an appropriate null hypothesis for this study?

options.A: The average number of hours of exercise per week for East Coast adults is different from the average number of hours of exercise per week for West Coast adults.
options.B: The average number of hours of exercise per week for East Coast adults is the same as the average number of hours of exercise per week for West Coast adults.
options.C: The average number of hours of exercise per week for East Coast adults is greater than the average number of hours of exercise per week for West Coast adults.
options.D: The average number of hours of exercise per week for East Coast adults is less than the average number of hours of exercise per week for West Coast adults.

text: For a roll of a fair die, each of the outcomes 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, or 6 is equally likely. A red die and a green die are rolled simultaneously, and the difference of the outcomes (red – green) is computed. This is repeated for a total of 500 rolls of the pair of dice. Which of the following graphs best represents the most reasonable distribution of the differences?

The probability of winning a certain game is 0.5. If at least 70 percent of the games in a series of n games are won, the player wins a prize. If the possible choices for n are =10, =20, n n and =100, n which value of n should the player choose in order to maximize the probability of winning a prize?

options.A: =10 n only
options.B: =20 n only
options.C: =100 n only
options.D: =10 n or =20 n only; the probabilities are the same.

text: Height, in meters, is measured for each person in a sample. After the data are collected, all the height measurements are converted from meters to centimeters by multiplying each measurement by 100. Which of the following statistics will remain the same for both units of measure?

options.A: The mean of the height measurements
options.B: The median of the height measurements
options.C: The standard deviation of the height measurements
options.D: The maximum of the height measurements

text: A school principal wanted to investigate student opinion about the food served in the school cafeteria. The principal selected at random samples of 50 first-year students, 50 second-year students, 50 third-year students, and 50 fourth-year students to complete a questionnaire. Which of the following best describes the principal’s sampling plan?

options.A: A stratified random sample
options.B: A simple random sample
options.C: A cluster sample
options.D: A convenience sample
options.E: A systematic sample

text: A candy company produces individually wrapped candies. The quality control manager for the company believes that the weight of the candies is approximately normally distributed with mean 720 milligrams (mg). If the manager’s belief is correct, which of the following intervals of weights will contain the largest proportion of the candies in the distribution of weights?

options.A: 740 mg to 780 mg
options.B: 700 mg to 740 mg
options.C: 680 mg to 720 mg
options.D: 660 mg to 700 mg

text: A company currently uses Brand A lightbulbs, which have a mean life of 1,000 hours. A salesperson marketing Brand B, a new brand of bulb, contacts the company. The company will switch to the new brand of bulb only if there is convincing evidence that the mean life of Brand B is greater than 1,000 hours. Which of the following hypotheses should the company test?

options.A: The mean life of Brand B bulbs is 1,000 hours. The mean life of Brand B bulbs is more than 1,000 hours.
options.B: The mean life of Brand B bulbs is 1,000 hours. The mean life of Brand B bulbs is less than 1,000 hours.
options.C: The mean life of Brand A bulbs is 1,000 hours. The mean life of Brand A bulbs is more than 1,000 hours.
options.D: The mean life of Brand A bulbs is 1,000 hours. The mean life of Brand A bulbs is less than 1,000 hours.

text: The amount of time required for each of 100 mice to navigate through a maze was recorded. The histogram below shows the distribution of times, in seconds, for the 100 mice. Which of the following values is closest to the standard deviation of the 100 times?

options.A: 2.5 seconds
options.B: 10 seconds
options.C: 20 seconds
options.D: 50 seconds

text: A graph (not shown) of the selling prices of homes in a certain city for the month of April reveals that the distribution is skewed to the left. Which of the following statements is the most reasonable conclusion about the selling prices based on the graph?

options.A: The mean is greater than the median.
options.B: The median is the average of the first quartile and the third quartile.
options.C: There are fewer selling prices between the first quartile and the median than there are between the median and the third quartile.
options.D: There are more selling prices that are less than the mean than selling prices that are greater than the mean.
options.E: The value of maximum minus third quartile is less than the value of first quartile minus minimum.

text: A survey was conducted in which both men and women were asked a question about a current issue. Possible responses to this question were “in favor of,” “not in favor of,” or “no opinion.” A chi-square test is to be used to determine whether the response to this question is independent of gender. The number of degrees of freedom for the chi-square test in this situation is
If a probability distribution is symmetric, which of the following statements must be true?

options.A: The distribution is normal.
options.B: The distribution is uniform.
options.C: The distribution is bimodal.
options.D: The mean of the distribution is equal to the median of the distribution.
options.E: The interquartile range of the distribution is equal to the standard deviation of the distribution.

text: Let X represent the number on the face that lands up when a fair six-sided number cube is tossed. The expected value of X is 3.5, and the standard deviation of X is approximately 1.708. Two fair six-sided number cubes will be tossed, and the numbers appearing on the faces that land up will be added. Which of the following values is closest to the standard deviation of the resulting sum?

Based on previous research, the standard deviation of the distribution of the age at which children begin to walk is estimated to be 1.5 months. A random sample of children will be selected, and the age at which each child begins to walk will be recorded. A 99 percent confidence interval for the average age at which children begin to walk will be constructed using the data obtained from the sample of children. Of the following, which is the smallest sample size that will result in a margin of error of 0.1 month or less for the confidence interval?
Let X be a random variable that has a skewed distribution with mean and standard deviation . Based on random samples of size 400, the sampling distribution of is

options.A: highly skewed with mean 10 and standard deviation 10
options.B: highly skewed with mean 10 and standard deviation 5
options.C: highly skewed with mean 10 and standard deviation 0.5
options.D: approximately normal with mean 10 and standard deviation 10

text: The number of hurricanes reaching the East Coast of the United States was recorded for each of the last ten decades by the National Hurricane Center. Summary measures are shown below. Min = 12 Max = 24 Lower quartile = 15 Upper quartile = 18 Median = 16 n = 10 Which of the following statements is true?

options.A: The smallest observation is 12 and it is an outlier. No other observations in the data set could be outliers.
options.B: The largest observation is 24 and it is an outlier. No other observations in the data set could be outliers.
options.C: Both 12 and 24 are outliers. It is possible that there are also other outliers.
options.D: 12 is an outlier and it is possible that there are other outliers at the low end of the data set. There are no outliers at the high end of the data set.

text: Makers of a new pain-relieving medication claim that it relieves chronic pain faster than the current top-selling pain reliever on the market. A double-blind experiment was conducted in which 10 people who experience chronic pain were randomly selected to take either the new or the current medication. Each of the 10 people recorded the time, in minutes, from taking the medication until pain relief. After an appropriate time period, each of the 10 people took the other medication and recorded the time from taking the medication until pain relief. The medication each person took first was randomly determined, and because both medications look the same, the people in the study did not know which medication was taken first. The table below shows summary statistics for the results. Minutes until Pain Relief Difference (new minus current) New Medication Current Medication Mean 15.600 16.025 Standard deviation 4.811 4.833 1.395 Which of the following values is closest to the p-value of the appropriate t-test?
Which of the following graphs represents a binomial distribution with and ?

An experiment will be conducted to determine whether children learn their multiplication facts better by practicing with flash cards or by practicing on a computer. Children who volunteer for the experiment will be randomly assigned to one of the two treatments. Because the children’s gender may affect the outcome, there will be blocking by gender. After practice, the children will be given a test on their multiplication facts. Why will it be impossible to conduct a double-blind experiment?

options.A: The experimenter will know whether the child is a boy or a girl and whether he or she used flash cards or the computer.
options.B: The child will know whether he or she is a boy or a girl.
options.C: The child will know whether he or she used flash cards or the computer.
options.D: The person who grades the tests will know whether the child was a boy or a girl.

text: A police officer is using a radar device to check motorists’ speeds. Prior to beginning the speed check, the officer estimates that 40 percent of motorists will be driving more than 5 miles per hour over the speed limit. Assuming that the police officer’s estimate is correct, what is the probability that among 4 randomly selected motorists, the officer will find at least 1 motorist driving more than 5 miles per hour over the speed limit?

Zucchini weights are approximately normally distributed with mean 0.8 pound and standard deviation 0.25 pound. Which of the following shaded regions best represents the probability that a randomly selected zucchini will weigh between 0.55 pound and 1.3 pounds?

In a recent survey, high school students and their parents were asked to rate 60 recently released movies. The ratings were on a scale from 1 to 9, where 1 was “horrible” and 9 was “excellent”. For each movie, the average rating by the students and the average rating by their parents was calculated and the scatterplot below was constructed. The horizontal axis represents the student rating, and the vertical axis represents the parent rating. Thus, an individual data point would represent the rating of a single movie. Which of the following statements is justified by the scatterplot?

options.A: The movies that the students liked the best also tended to be the movies that the parents liked the best, but the students tended to give lower scores.
options.B: The movies that the students liked the best also tended to be the movies that the parents liked the best, but the students tended to give higher scores.
options.C: The movies that the students liked the best also tended to be the movies that the parents liked the best, but each group tended to give the same scores.

options.A: The population proportion will be in the confidence interval.
options.B: The probability that the confidence interval will include the population proportion is 0.99.
options.C: The probability that the confidence interval will include the sample proportion is 0.99.
options.D: The population proportion and the sample proportion will be equal.

text: Three brands of candy pieces—X, Y, and Z—are made in many colors. Shaela bought one bag of each brand and counted the number of pieces of each color. The graph below shows the relative frequency distribution of colors for each bag. Which of the following statements must be true?

options.A: For Brand X, there were more green candy pieces than red candy pieces in the bag.
options.B: For Brand Y, there were more red candy pieces than green candy pieces in the bag.
options.C: There were more green candy pieces in the Brand X bag than were in the Brand Z bag.
options.D: There were the same number of blue candy pieces in the Brand X bag as were in the Brand Y bag.

text: A large city newspaper periodically reports the mean cost of dinner for two people at restaurants in the city. The newspaper staff will collect data from a random sample of restaurants in the city and estimate the mean price using a 90 percent confidence interval. In past years, the standard deviation has always been very close to $35. Assuming that the population standard deviation is $35, which of the following is the minimum sample size needed to obtain a margin of error of no more than $5 ?

A field researcher who studies lions conjectured that the more time a cub spends playing, the sooner the cub will begin to hunt. Observational data were collected from 20 lion cubs. The researcher recorded how long they spent playing and the age when they began hunting. Because male and female lions have different hunting behaviors, the researcher recorded the data for males and females separately. The two scatterplots show the data for the 10 female lions and the 10 male lions. Based on the scatterplots, for which gender does there appear to be evidence that the more time a lion cub spends playing, the sooner the cub is likely to begin hunting?

options.A: For female cubs only
options.B: For male cubs only
options.C: For both male cubs and female cubs, with equal evidence
options.D: For both male cubs and female cubs, with more evidence for female cubs than for male cubs

text: A 95 percent confidence interval for the mean time, in minutes, for a volunteer fire company to respond to emergency incidents is determined to be (2.8, 12.3). Which of the following is the best interpretation of the interval?

options.A: Five percent of the time, the time for response is less than 2.8 minutes or greater than 12.3 minutes.
options.B: The probability is 0.95 that a randomly selected time for response will be between 2.8 minutes and 12.3 minutes.
options.C: Ninety-five percent of the time the mean time for response is between 2.8 minutes and 12.3 minutes.
options.D: We are 95% confident that the mean time for response is between 2.8 minutes and 12.3 minutes.
options.E: We are 95% confident that a randomly selected time for response will be between 2.8 minutes and 12.3 minutes.

text: Researchers will conduct a study of the television-viewing habits of children. options.D: A sample of size 25 will produce a more biased estimator than a sample of size 50.

A machine is designed to dispense at least 12 ounces of a beverage into a bottle. To test whether the machine is working properly, a random sample of 50 bottles was selected and the mean number of ounces for the 50 bottles was computed. A test of the hypotheses H : m = versus m < a H : 12 was conducted, where m represents the population mean number of ounces of the beverage dispensed per bottle by the machine. The p-value for the test was 0.08. Which of the following is the most appropriate conclusion to draw at the significance level of a = 0.05 ?

options.A: Because the p-value is greater than the significance level, there is convincing evidence that the population mean number of ounces dispensed into a bottle is 12 ounces.
options.B: Because the p-value is greater than the significance level, there is convincing evidence that the population mean number of ounces dispensed into a bottle is less than 12 ounces.
options.C: Because the p-value is greater than the significance level, there is not convincing evidence that the population mean number of ounces dispensed into a bottle is less than 12 ounces.
options.D: Because the p-value is less than the significance level, there is convincing evidence that the population mean number of ounces dispensed into a bottle is 12 ounces.

text: In a certain school, students can choose whether to eat in the school’s cafeteria. A reporter working for the school’s newspaper polled students on their reactions to changes in the menu at the cafeteria. For each student leaving the cafeteria in one 30-minute time period, the reporter used a coin to determine whether to stop the student and ask how he or she felt about the new menu. In the reporter’s article it was stated that a random sample of the students showed that 89 percent of the school’s student population was happy with the new menu. Which of the following statements is true?

options.A: Because each student leaving the cafeteria was randomly selected and could choose to answer or not, this is a random sample of the student population, and the 89% is an accurate measurement of the school population’s view of the new menu.
options.B: Because students self-selected whether to eat in the cafeteria, the sampling method might be biased and the sample might not be representative of all students in the school.
options.C: The survey would have been more effective if the reporter had collected the data in one 15-minute time period rather than in one 30-minute time period.
options.D: The survey would have been more effective if students who cared about the food could have called the reporter to tell how they felt about the new menu, so that only students with opinions on the subject would have been surveyed.

text: Ms. Tucker travels through two intersections with traffic lights as she drives to the market. The traffic lights operate independently. What is the probability that the second light will be red when she reaches it?

text: A representative of a car manufacturer in the United States made the following claim in a news report. Ten years ago, only 53 percent of Americans owned American-made cars, but that figure is significantly higher today. A research group conducted a study to investigate whether the claim was true. The group found that 56 percent of a randomly selected sample of car owners in the United States owned American-made cars. A test of the appropriate hypotheses resulted in a p-value of 0.283. Assuming the conditions for inference were met, is there sufficient evidence to conclude, at the significance level of alpha equals 0.05, that the proportion of all car owners in the United States who own American-made cars has increased from what it was ten years ago?

options.A: Yes, because 0.56 is greater than 0.53.
options.B: Yes, because a reasonable interval for the proportion is 0.56 plus or minus 0.283.
options.C: Yes, becasue 0.56 min us 0 . 3 equals 0.03 a nd 0.03 is less than 0.05.

text: Researchers wanted to investigate whether babies have a sense of right and wrong. They showed each of 60 babies a puppet show in which a red puppet was trying to open a heavy box lid. A second puppet, called the helper, would try to help the red puppet open the box, while a third puppet, called the hinderer, would try to slam the box lid down. After watching the show, each baby was presented with a tray containing the helper puppet and the hinderer puppet, and the researchers recorded which puppet the baby reached for. The researchers wanted to determine whether the babies would reach for the helper puppet more than for the hinderer puppet. As part of the show, a green puppet and a yellow puppet served as the helper and hinderer. For each baby, a coin was tossed to determine which color would serve which role. Which of the following is the most important reason for the random assignment of color to role in the study?

options.A: Slamming the lid might cause wear on the hinderer puppet after 60 shows. The random assignment of color to role permits more even wear between the helper and the hinderer.
options.B: The puppeteer might grow tired of doing the same show with the same puppet colors. The random assignment of color to role keeps the show fresh for each performance.
options.C: If the same role is played by the same color puppet, the babies might show a preference for the color instead of a preference for the role.
options.D: The random assignment of color to role allows the researchers to determine if one color is better than another in teaching babies right from wrong.

A contestant’s final winnings on a game show are determined by a random selection of a base amount and a possible multiplier. The ability to visually search, such as when reading an x-ray or interpreting a satellite image, is an important skill in many jobs. (c) When the appropriate test was performed, no significant difference was found in mean improvement time between the driving game and the sports game. text: Students in an online course are each randomly assigned to receive either standard practice exercises or adaptive practice exercises. Two students, Joshua and Mateo, from this league competed in the long jump. Joshua’s distance was 0.52 standard deviation below the mean of the distribution. options.A: Joshua’s distance was at the 30th percentile of the distribution and was less than Mateo’s distance.
options.B: Joshua’s distance was at the 30th percentile of the distribution and was greater than Mateo’s distance.
options.C: Joshua’s distance was at the 48th percentile of the distribution and was less than Mateo’s distance.
options.D: Joshua’s distance was at the 48th percentile of the distribution and was greater than Mateo’s distance.
options.E: Joshua’s distance was at the 70th percentile of the distribution and was greater than Mateo’s distance.

text: A large manufacturing company has tens of thousands of employees, and 30 percent of the employees have completed a management training course. Suppose a simple random sample of 50 employees is selected. Which of the following is closest to the probability that more than half of the sample will be employees who have completed the management training course?

The recreation committee of a certain town is interested in creating a new nature trail for walkers, runners, and bikers. A random sample of 100 town residents were asked whether they are in favor of creating the new trail. Of those sampled, 78 said yes. What is the population of interest for which the committee can generalize its conclusions?

options.A: The 100 town residents who were asked about whether they are in favor of creating the new trail
options.B: The 78 town residents who responded that they are in favor of creating the new trail
options.C: All town residents who are walkers, runners, or bikers
options.D: All town residents who are in favor of the new trail
options.E: All town residents

text: Carol, a reporter in a large city, took a random sample of 300 households and found that the 90 percent confidence interval for the proportion of households that own a pet is (0.63,0.71). Jason, the director of a local animal shelter, wants to verify this result for the proportion of households that own a pet and takes a different random sample of 200 households and finds that 67 percent own a pet. Assume the conditions for inference have been met. Jason will calculate a 90 percent confidence interval for the proportion of households that own a pet using his sample data. How would the margin of error from Jason’s confidence interval compare to the margin of error from Carol’s confidence interval?

options.A: Jason’s margin of error would be the same as Carol’s margin of error because the confidence level did not change.
options.B: Jason’s margin of error would be the same as Carol’s margin of error because the point estimate did not change.
options.C: Jason’s margin of error would be less than Carol’s margin of error because Jason used a smaller sample size.
options.D: Jason’s margin of error would be greater than Carol’s margin of error because Jason used a smaller sample size.
options.E: Jason’s margin of error would be greater than Carol’s margin of error because Jason’s point estimate is greater.

text: A botanist is studying the weights, measured in pounds, of pumpkins grown at two different farms. The boxplots show the distribution of pumpkin weight for two samples of equal size, one from farm A and the other from farm B. Based on the boxplots, which of the following is a correct conclusion about the distribution of pumpkin weight for the samples from the two farms?

options.A: The interquartile ranges of the pumpkin weights are the same for farm A and farm B.
options.B: The range of the pumpkin weights for farm B is greater than the range for farm A.
options.C: There are more pumpkin weights that are greater than 18 pounds from farm A than there are from farm B.
options.D: There are more pumpkin weights that are greater than 12 pounds from farm B than there are from farm A.
options.E: There are more pumpkin weights that are less than 10 pounds from farm B than there are from farm A.

text: Voters in a certain large county will have an opportunity to vote for or against a new environmental rule that would incentivize the return of certain used batteries to a county-certified recycling facility. The results of a previous poll found that 41 percent of registered voters in that county plan to vote for the environmental rule. Juwan, an engineer working at the recycling facility, randomly selected 15 registered voters and asked whether they plan to vote for the rule. Assuming that 41 percent represents the actual percent of registered voters in the county who plan to vote for the environmental rule, which of the following is closest to the probability that at most 2 of the registered voters Juwan selected plan to vote for the environmental rule?

A data scientist had different 5 sets of data. For each data set the scientist fit a least-squares regression line, then graphed the residuals versus the predicted values for each. Which residual plot indicates that the least-squares regression model was the most appropriate model to fit the data?

A jewelry manufacturer tracks sales of three types of jewelry (rings, necklaces, and earrings) across each of three different retail outlet categories (specialty store, department store, and warehouse club). The segmented bar chart shows the distribution for each retail outlet for the three jewelry types based on sales data for the past week. Based on the chart, which statement must be true?

options.A: The percent of earring sales at department stores was less than the percent of necklace sales at specialty stores.
options.B: For each retail outlet category, less than 30% of sales were earrings.
options.C: The number of earring sales for department stores was the same as the number of earring sales for warehouse clubs.
options.D: The greatest number of ring sales came from warehouse clubs.
options.E: The number of ring sales for specialty stores was greater than the number of ring sales for warehouse clubs.

text: A one-sample z-test for a population proportion was conducted to test H0 : p = 0.35 versus Ha : p ̸= 0.35. Assuming the conditions for inference were met, which combination of sample proportion and sample size would result in the smallest p-value of the test?

options.A: ˆp = 0.35 and n = 200

options.C: ˆp = 0.40 and n = 60

text: Two scatterplots are shown. Based on the scatterplots, which statement CANNOT be true?

options.B: Plot B indicates a linear relationship between the variables.

options.D: The correlation coefficient for the data in plot A is close to 0.
options.E: The r2 value of the least-squares regression line for plot B is greater than the r2 value of the least-squares regression line for plot A.

text: Researchers studying the relationship between screen time and developmental scores of children in Canada selected a random sample of 2,441 children. For each child, the researchers recorded the child’s screen time, in hours per day, when they were 2 years old (x) and their scores on a developmental test, in points, when they were 5 years old (y). Higher scores on the developmental test are better. The 95 percent confidence interval for the slope of the least-squares regression line for predicting scores on the developmental test based on screen time was (−0.13,−0.02). The conditions for inference were met, and the researchers concluded that increased screen time is associated with lower scores on the developmental test. Which of the following provides the best reason that supports the researchers’ conclusion?

options.A: This was a 95% confidence interval, which is a high level of confidence.
options.B: The sample size was large.
options.C: The researchers took years to complete the study.
options.D: The confidence interval for the slope of the regression line does not contain 0.
options.E: The confidence interval for the slope of the regression line consists entirely of negative numbers.

text: To investigate whether more than half the adults in a country are fearful of riding in a self-driving car, researchers surveyed a random sample of 1,600 adults in the country. Of those in the sample, 1,280 adults responded that they would be fearful of riding in a self-driving car. Which of the following represents the value of the test statistic for the appropriate hypothesis test for the investigation?

Researchers will conduct an experiment to compare the effectiveness of two brands of shampoo—Brand X and Brand Y. They will use a matched-pairs design in which each participant will use one brand for one week and then the other brand for one week. For each participant, the researchers will randomly assign which shampoo, Brand X or Brand Y, will be used first, and 15 milliliters of shampoo will be used for each hair wash. Because the temperature of the water is known to have an effect, a water temperature less than 100◦F will be used for the first week and a water temperature greater than 100◦F will be used for the second week. At the end of each week, the effectiveness of the shampoo being used will be measured. Which of the following would improve this matched-pairs experiment?

options.A: Have each participant use both brands, but have all participants use Brand X first, then switch to Brand Y
options.B: Have each participant use only one of the shampoo brands, with brand randomly assigned for each participant
options.C: Have each participant use only one of the shampoo brands, with brand determined by the subject’s preference
options.D: Let each participant decide for themselves how much shampoo to use
options.E: Let each participant decide for themselves what water temperature to use, but each participant must keep the water temperature the same for both brands

text: Gwyneth measured the weights, in pounds, of ten striped bass taken from a local river. She calculated a mean weight of 13 pounds and a range of 5 pounds. For a report, Gwyneth converted the measurements from pounds to kilograms. (Note: There is approximately 0.454 kilogram in 1 pound.) Which of the following is closest to the mean and range of the converted weights?

options.A: The mean weight is 28.63 kilograms, and the range is 5 kilograms.
options.B: The mean weight is 28.63 kilograms, and the range is 2.27 kilograms.

options.E: The mean weight is 5.90 kilograms, and the range is 2.27 kilograms.

text: Karmen and Sophia are on a swim team and race in different events at a swim meet. The probability that Karmen will win her race at the swim meet is 0.1. The probability that Sophia will win her race at the swim meet is 0.3. If the events of Karmen winning her race and Sophia winning her race are independent, what is the probability that either Karmen or Sophia will win her race at the swim meet?

Daniela designed an experiment to investigate whether listening to music or burning a scented candle affects her performance while playing her favorite video game. According to a survey, 32 percent of people in a certain country regularly recycle. Mr. Giovanni, the mayor of a specific city in the country, wanted to know whether the residents in his city regularly recycle more than the national average. He selected a random sample of 50 residents, 28 of whom regularly recycle. Mr. Giovanni would like to use a one-sample z-test for a population proportion to test the hypotheses $H_0: p = 0.32$ versus $H_a: p > 0.32$, where $p$ is the proportion of city residents who regularly recycle. In order to use this test, the sampling distribution of the sample proportion needs to be approximately normal. Which of the following is true for Mr. Giovanni's study and ensures that the sampling distribution of the sample proportion is approximately normal?

options.A: The distribution of the population of all residents who regularly recycle is approximately normal.
options.B: The sample size is 50, which is larger than 30.
options.C: The sample size of 50 is less than 10% of the residents in the city.

options.E: The observed number of residents who regularly recycle is 28, and the observed number of residents who do not regularly recycle is 22, and both numbers are at least 10.

text: A group of farmers wants to estimate the mean length of carrots grown on their farms. The farmers will select a random sample of carrots from the farms and will measure each carrot's length, in inches, and compute the mean and standard deviation of the sample. Assuming the conditions for inference are met, which of the following is the most appropriate method to use for such an estimate?

options.A: A one-sample $t$-interval for a population mean
options.B: A one-sample $t$-interval for a sample mean
options.C: A one-sample $z$-interval for a population mean
options.D: A one-sample $z$-interval for a population proportion
options.E: A one-sample $z$-interval for a sample proportion

text: Scores from a robotics competition have a mean of 19.9 points and a standard deviation of 7.2 points. Scores from an art competition have a mean of 20.2 points and a standard deviation of 5.7 points. Dominic enters both competitions and gets a score of 27 in robotics and a score of 26 in art. Based on Dominic’s standardized scores, in which competition did Dominic have a better performance?

options.A: Robotics, because Dominic’s standardized score in robotics is approximately 0.99, while his standardized score in art is approximately 0.85.
options.B: Robotics, because Dominic’s standardized score in robotics is approximately 1.19, while his standardized score in art is approximately 1.02.
options.C: Art, because Dominic’s standardized score in art is approximately 1.19, while his standardized score in robotics is approximately 0.85.
options.D: Art, because Dominic’s standardized score in art is approximately 1.02, while his standardized score in robotics is approximately 0.99.
options.E: Art, because Dominic’s standardized score in art is approximately 0.99, while his standardized score in robotics is approximately 1.02.

text: A professional sports league has 30 teams. A fan of one of the teams is interested in the mean yearly salary of players on that team. The fan obtains the yearly salary of each player for all 28 players on the team. Which of the following best describes the fan’s sampling plan?

options.A: Simple random sample
options.B: Stratified random sample, with teams as strata
options.C: Cluster sample, with teams as clusters
options.D: Systematic random sample
options.E: Census

text: In an experiment, Jaden, a statistics student, wants to investigate whether listening to music while playing a game of chess results in a game with more total moves. Jaden's large college has 200 students who play chess and are willing to participate in the experiment. He allows the players to decide whether they will play a chess game against a computer program while listening to music or will play a chess game against the same computer program while not listening to music. The total number of moves it takes for each chess game will be recorded. Which of the following statements best describes why this is not a well-designed experiment?

options.A: The subjects cannot be blind to the treatment because they will know whether they are listening to music.
options.B: The treatments were not randomly assigned.
options.C: There is not a control group of players who do not play chess but do listen to music.
options.D: There was no replication because each player plays only one game against the computer program.
options.E: The players were not randomly selected.

text: In a certain game, a player wins a prize by randomly picking a red marble from a bag of differently colored marbles. The probability that a player will win a prize on a given play of the game is 0.26. Assuming successive plays of the game are independent, which of the following statements is true?

options.A: If a player plays this game a very large number of times, the player will win a prize about 26% of the time.
options.B: If a player plays the game 100 times, the player will win a prize exactly 26 times.
options.C: If a player does not win a prize in 25 consecutive plays, the player is guaranteed to win a prize on the 26th play.
options.D: It is impossible for the player to win a prize on two consecutive plays of the game.
options.E: The player is guaranteed to win exactly one prize in 26 consecutive plays of the game.

text: A simple random sample of $n = 23$ people who commute from home to work in a large metropolitan area is selected. Each person is asked their distance, in miles, from home to work and how long it typically takes them, in minutes, to commute from home to work. The distance, $x$, and typical commute time, $y$, are recorded for each person in the sample. A least-squares regression model using distance to predict typical commute time results in the following computer output. Regression Output | Predictor | Coefficient | SE of Coefficient | T | P-Value | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Constant | 5.514 | 1.805 | 3.06 | 0.0063 | | Distance | 1.216 | 0.103 | 11.86 | < 0.0001 | | $S = 5.610$ | $r^2 = 0.875$ | | | | Assuming all conditions for inference are met, which of the following is a 90 percent confidence interval for the predicted change in typical commute time for a one-mile increase in the distance from home to work?

Blue light is the wavelength of light given off by electronic devices and energy-efficient lightbulbs. Researchers are interested in investigating the effect of blue light on sleep. The researchers conduct a study using 30 volunteers. There are two light settings used in the study: one with a high amount of blue light and one with a low amount of blue light. Each volunteer will spend the night in the researchers' sleep lab on two different nights. On one of the nights, each volunteer will be exposed to one of the two light settings for 50 minutes prior to their usual bedtime. On another night, each volunteer will be exposed to the other light setting for 50 minutes prior to their usual bedtime. The light setting that is experienced first is randomized for each volunteer. After each night in the sleep lab, the amount of melatonin, a hormone associated with the sleep-wake cycle, is measured for each volunteer. When measuring the results, the researchers know which light setting each volunteer was exposed to each night. Which experimental design or method are the researchers using for the study?

options.A: Double-blind experiment
options.B: Randomized complete block design, blocked by usual bedtime
options.C: Completely randomized design
options.D: Matched pairs design
options.E: This is an observational study, not an experiment.

text: A researcher in a certain country claims that the proportion of people in their twenties (people aged 20 to 29) who can lift more than 200 pounds is greater than the proportion of people in their forties (people aged 40 to 49) who can lift more than 200 pounds. In a random sample of people in their twenties living in that country, 31 percent could lift more than 200 pounds. In a separate random sample of people in their forties living in that country, 22 percent could lift more than 200 pounds. Let $p_1$ be the proportion of all people in their twenties living in the country who can lift more than 200 pounds. Let $p_2$ be the proportion of all people in their forties living in the country who can lift more than 200 pounds. Which of the following are the appropriate null and alternative hypotheses for testing the researcher’s claim for these two age-groups?

Many cities are hiring artists to create asphalt art, in which artists paint murals directly on certain roads in hopes of getting people to slow down and follow the speed limit along those roads. During a 24-hour period, a researcher measured the speeds of all drivers who drove on randomly selected roads. Some roads had art, and some roads did not have art. Speed on Roads Based on the boxplots, which of the following is a true statement?

options.A: Because the speed of the first quartile on the roads with art is less than the minimum speed on the roads without art, it is not likely that drivers on roads with art travel at slower speeds.

options.C: Because the interquartile range of the speeds on the roads with art is less than the interquartile range of the speeds on the roads without art, it is likely that drivers on roads with art travel at slower speeds.
options.D: Because the maximum speed on the roads with art is greater than the median speed on the roads without art, it is likely that drivers on roads with art travel at slower speeds.

text: Spam e-mail (unwanted electronic mail) is usually sent in bulk to a large list of recipients. A large e-mail service provider estimates that 4 percent of all e-mails are spam. In a random sample of 50 e-mails processed by the service provider, which of the following is the approximate probability that the sample will have at least 3 spam e-mails?

A company owns a group of 50 cars, all the same make, model, and year. The group has a mean fuel economy μ = 23.2 miles per gallon (mpg) and a standard deviation σ = 2.4 mpg. options.A: Mean = 23.2 mpg, standard deviation = 2.4 mpg
options.B: Mean = 23.2 mpg, standard deviation = 1.2 mpg

options.D: Mean = 11.6 mpg, standard deviation = 2.4 mpg

text: A factory that manufactures glass has two shifts of workers: a daytime shift and an evening shift. A random sample of 200 pieces of glass was selected from each shift, and the pieces were carefully inspected for defects. From the daytime shift, 12 pieces of glass were defective. From the evening shift, 15 pieces of glass were defective. Assuming all conditions for inference are met, which of the following gives a 98 percent confidence interval for the difference (daytime shift minus evening shift) in the population proportion of defective glass for the two shifts?

Information obtained from a random sample of 40 entry-level lawyers working in a certain country was used to calculate a 95 percent confidence interval for the true mean salary of all entry-level lawyers in the country. The confidence interval is ($56,100, $58,900). Assume the conditions for inference are met. Which of the following statements is correct?

text: A number cube has six sides, each with equal probability of landing face-up when rolled, which is also known as a fair number cube. A statistics teacher is trying to create a six-sided number cube where each side does not have an equal probability of landing face-up when rolled.
